content: Hey, I've been dealing with this right shoulder issue for about 3 weeks now. My physio Dr. Park gave me some exercises to do at home, but I'm not totally sure I'm doing them right. The external rotation with the resistance band feels pretty uncomfortable when I get to around the 12th rep. Is that normal, or am I pushing too hard? My pain is usually a 3 out of 10 when I'm just sitting, but it gets way worse—like a 6—when I try to reach overhead or lift anything.

That's really helpful context. So here's the thing—some discomfort during exercises is normal when you're rehabbing a shoulder, but there's a difference between good therapeutic discomfort and pain that means you're pushing too hard. For the external rotation with the band, you should aim for about a 4-5 out of 10 discomfort max while doing it. If you're hitting 6+ by rep 12, that suggests a few possibilities: maybe the band resistance is too strong, your form might be drifting, or you could be fatiguing the supporting muscles. I'd definitely mention this to Dr. Park at your next session and possibly scale back to a lighter band. Also, the fact that overhead movements spike your pain to 6/10 is pretty typical for shoulder issues—your body is protecting the joint. Are you doing the pendulum swings and doorway stretches daily too, or mainly just the band work?

Yeah, I'm trying to do all three exercises every day. The pendulum swings feel pretty good actually—almost soothing—but the doorway stretch is weird. Like, sometimes it feels like it's actually helping loosen things up, and other times it feels like I'm just making the pain worse. How long should I be holding that stretch? Dr. Park said 30 seconds but didn't specify if I should do it multiple times. Also, should I be doing these exercises before or after work? I've got a desk job so I'm hunched over a computer most of the time.

Good question about timing—actually, for a desk job that's creating postural stress, you'd benefit from doing some of these exercises *during* the day, not just at home. The doorway stretch in particular is great as a break from being hunched. For that one, 30 seconds is solid, but you should do 2-3 repetitions of it throughout the day if possible. The key with the doorway stretch is that it shouldn't feel sharp or acutely painful—if it does, you might be forcing the stretch too aggressively. Gentle tension is what you're looking for. The pendulum swings are actually ideal to do mid-workday because they're low-impact and actually help mobilize the joint without loading it. I'd suggest doing the full resistance band routine (3 sets of 15) once daily, ideally in the evening when you're not fatigued from work, but break up the stretches throughout the day. Also, since you're desk-bound, try setting a timer to get up every hour and do 1-2 sets of pendulum swings and the doorway stretch—it'll help counteract that forward shoulder posture you're probably developing.

That's really helpful. I'm going to try breaking up the stretches during the day. One more thing—I'm supposed to have a follow-up with Dr. Park in about 2 weeks, and I want to show real progress. Like, should I be tracking my pain levels or any specific metrics? And honestly, how long does something like this usually take to get better? I'm getting a bit anxious about it because my job sometimes involves lifting boxes or reaching up to shelves, and I don't want this to become a chronic thing.

Tracking is absolutely worth doing—keep a simple daily log of your resting pain level and your worst pain level that day, plus maybe a note on how your overhead movements felt. That gives Dr. Park concrete data to work with and helps you see progress that might be subtle day-to-day. For timelines, shoulder injuries like yours typically show noticeable improvement within 4-6 weeks if you're consistent with physio, but full recovery often takes 8-12 weeks depending on severity. The good news is you're only 3 weeks in, so you're still in that early phase. Regarding your job—definitely talk to Dr. Park about modifying your work activities temporarily. You probably shouldn't be lifting heavy boxes or reaching overhead until you've got that pain down to 1-2/10 in those movements. Most people can return to normal activity gradually if they don't re-injure themselves by pushing too hard too soon. The anxiety is understandable, but shoulder injuries are very manageable with proper rehab. When you see Dr. Park in 2 weeks, bring your pain log and ask specifically about returning to those work tasks—he might give you modified versions you can do safely.

Perfect, I'll start keeping a log today. Quick question before I forget—is ice or heat better for this? Sometimes after I do my exercises my shoulder feels a bit hot and aggravated. And should I be taking any anti-inflammatories regularly, or just when the pain is really bad?

Good instinct asking about this. So for shoulder injuries in the rehab phase, ice right after exercises (15-20 minutes) is usually better, especially if you're feeling that post-exercise inflammation. Ice helps manage swelling that can actually restrict your movement and slow progress. Save heat for when your muscles feel stiff—like mornings or after being stationary for a while—since heat helps with mobility. For anti-inflammatories, I'd say ice is your first line of defense before reaching for medication. NSAIDs like ibuprofen can help in the short term if you're having a particularly painful flare-up, but you shouldn't take them routinely without checking with Dr. Park or your doctor first. Some physios prefer you to let mild inflammation do its job because it's part of the healing process. Since your resting pain is only 3/10, you probably don't need regular anti-inflammatories—save them for when you really need them. That said, definitely ask Dr. Park at your next appointment about his specific preferences since he knows your exact diagnosis. He might have specific recommendations based on what's actually going on structurally with your shoulder.

Got it, I'll prioritize ice and ask Dr. Park about the anti-inflammatories. One last thing—I've been trying to sleep normally but I've noticed my right shoulder is pretty uncomfortable at night, especially if I sleep on my right side. Is there any sleeping position I should be favoring? And should I maybe get one of those specialized pillows or a support pillow?

Sleeping on the injured side is definitely going to aggravate it, so switching to your left side or back is wise for now. If you sleep on your back, a small pillow under your right arm to support it in a neutral position is helpful—you want to avoid your arm dangling or rotating. For sleeping on your left side, use a pillow between your knees (spinal alignment) and keep your right arm supported in front of you, not pinned under your body. You don't necessarily need an expensive specialty pillow—just a regular pillow under your arm works. Some people find a body pillow helpful for side sleeping because it gives you something to hug without overloading the shoulder. The key is keeping your shoulder in a relaxed, neutral position throughout the night. Pain at night can actually slow healing, so if you're waking up uncomfortable, that's worth adjusting. Since you're only 3 weeks in, this positioning issue should improve as your strength and stability improve—it won't be forever. Mention the nighttime discomfort to Dr. Park too, since sometimes shoulder pain patterns at night can indicate swelling or indicate if you need to modify how aggressively you're doing exercises that day.

That's really helpful—I'm feeling way more confident about managing this now. I'm going to set up my tracking log tonight, reorganize my sleeping setup, and be more intentional about spreading the stretches throughout my workday. I'll also hold off on the band exercises if the pain spikes above that 4-5 range you mentioned. One thing though—I'm seeing Dr. Park again in 2 weeks and I'm wondering if there are any questions I should definitely ask her during that appointment?

Great questions to bring to Dr. Park: ask about your timeline for returning to lifting and overhead work activities, whether your exercises should progress or change based on your improvement, and get her assessment of whether your pain trajectory is on track. Also mention that nighttime discomfort and ask if your sleeping position adjustments are sufficient or if she recommends anything else. Bring your pain log—she'll find that really useful and it shows you're taking your rehab seriously. You might also ask if there are any warning signs you should watch for that would mean you need to pull back, or whether you should continue ice after exercises or adjust that. And definitely clarify what 'discomfort vs. pain' should feel like during your exercises so you're on the same page. Finally, if you've been wondering about the root cause (like whether this was a rotator cuff strain, impingement, etc.), that's worth asking about too—understanding what actually happened helps you avoid re-injury. You seem really engaged with your recovery, so Dr. Park will probably appreciate how thoughtful you're being about it. Good luck with everything!
